nativeRange.habitat: Clear waters on well-developed coral reefs, reef channels connecting lagoons to the open ocean, and seaward reef slopes; reported at depths of roughly 3–40 m

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented feeding primarily on sponges and tunicates supplemented by algae. In captivity, tends to accept a varied diet including high-quality angelfish preparations containing sponge material, frozen mysis and brine shrimp, marine algae, and spirulina. Feeding small portions two to three times daily is generally advised to reduce the likelihood of coral nipping.

summary: A striking large angelfish from the Central Pacific, recognized by its golden-spotted brown body, blue lips, and black-edged fins. Generally considered intermediate in care and best kept singly in a well-established reef with ample live rock.
careNotes:
The Goldflake Angelfish is generally considered intermediate in difficulty and has been reported to reach around 25 cm (approximately 10 inches) as an adult. It tends to be semi-aggressive and territorial once established, and is best housed one per tank; conspecific and inter-angelfish aggression can be significant. A mature, well-established reef aquarium with plenty of live rock for grazing and several caves or crevices is generally advised. Moderate to strong water flow is commonly recommended to simulate natural reef conditions.

This species is generally considered reef-safe with caution: most individuals have been reported to leave corals undisturbed, but isolated cases of nipping at clam mantles (particularly Tridacna), LPS and SPS corals, and some soft corals have been documented. Captive-bred specimens tend to show fewer nipping behaviours than wild-caught individuals.

Lifespan reports vary considerably across sources — one source reports as few as 5 years, while others suggest 10–15 years in well-kept aquariums; the wide discrepancy likely reflects both variable husbandry quality and limited captive longevity data for this historically rare species.

Minimum tank size recommendations show significant spread across sources (75–211 US gallons / 284–800 L). Hobby-focused retailers cluster around 125–130 US gallons (~473–492 L), while a specialist aquarium database recommends 800 L, noting the fish requires ample open swimming space. A minimum of 500 L (approximately 130 US gallons) is adopted here as a cautious middle estimate, erring larger given the fish's active nature and 25 cm adult size; reviewers may wish to increase this further.

This species is a protogynous hermaphrodite (female-first sequential sex change), as is typical for the family Pomacanthidae. It is diurnal. The fish is historically rare in the trade but has become increasingly available, including captive-bred specimens.

RESOLVED 2026-06-03: fish.sexChange was incorrectly set to "protandrous". Corrected to "protogynous". The careNotes in this file already stated the species is a protogynous hermaphrodite, and the already-cited USGS source explicitly confirms: "Species in this family that have been studied are protogynous hermaphrodites, starting life female and later changing sex into a male." FishBase and Wikipedia were also consulted but do not provide species-specific hermaphroditism data for A. xanthopunctatus; the USGS and family-level biology (Pomacanthidae are protogynous) are sufficient to resolve this with confidence.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is rarely reported and is considered very difficult; commercial captive-bred availability is limited. No established captive breeding protocol is documented in mainstream hobbyist or scientific literature.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size, temperament, reef-safe status, native range. Gave minimum tank as 75 gal vs LiveAquaria 130 gal — wide disagreement; final value biased larger per playbook rules.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (74–82°F), pH (8.1–8.5), salinity (1.020–1.026), tank size (125 gal), care level (intermediate/moderate). Lifespan listed as 5 years.

additional.name: ReefApp
additional.notes: Specialist database recommends 800 L minimum — substantially larger than hobby sources. Confirmed adult size 25 cm and semi-aggressive temperament. Used to anchor the larger end of tank-size range.

additional.name: USGS Nonindigenous Aquatic Species
additional.notes: Confirmed native range (Caroline, Gilbert, Phoenix, Line Islands), adult size 25 cm, protogynous hermaphroditism, diurnal behaviour, and sponge/tunicate diet.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding is rarely reported; no established protocol exists. Commercial captive-bred availability is limited but growing.
